text: The surgeon identifies the hernia margins and begins preperitoneal or retromuscular dissection. Epiploic fat from the colon involved in the hernia is visible, along with the small bowel.
The surgeon begins developing a surgical flap, with visible bowel still partially attached to the hernia on the sidewall.

The surgeon performs a pre-transversalis dissection, which appears slightly bloody, then transitions to a pre-peritoneal approach. Cephalad to the hernia, the peritoneum thins, prompting a quick switch to pre-transversalis retromuscular dissection.

The surgeon develops the surgical space by manipulating the tissue, revealing a combination of transversus abdominis and diaphragm fat. The incision is made into the hernia sac as the surgeon approaches the hernia margins.
The surgeon begins dissection around the neck of the hernia, ensuring a safe approach to facilitate closure of the posterior aspect.
The surgeon dissects diaphragmatic fibers away from the transversalis fascia and peritoneum, creating a space on the opposite side of the hernia.

The surgeon moves caudally, unifying the dissection plane while working in the preperitoneal space. On the contralateral side, the dissection is in the retromuscular pre-transversalis plane, proceeding with a straightforward technique.

The surgeon identifies the volcano sign and begins reducing the hernia sac with minimal manipulation of the bowel. The hernia is visually confirmed as a true intraparietal hernia, with anterior muscle fibers likely from the internal and/or external oblique visible.

The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection around the hernia and proceeds to take down the remaining hernia sac, which includes some muscle tissue. The dissection is performed in a subdiaphragmatic plane, preparing to close the defect and reinforce the abdominal wall with a large mesh.
The surgeon completes a circumferential dissection around the hernia defect, revealing the bony prominences of the pelvis. The next step involves initiating the closure of the hernia defect.
The surgeon uses a non-absorbable V-lock suture to placate and close the hernia defect, incorporating some anterior tissue. No drain is placed to minimize the potential for an interparietal seroma.
The surgeon performs sequential closure by tightening sutures to re-approximate the edges of the transversus abdominis and diaphragm muscles.
The surgeon runs the suture back over itself to lock it and further plicate the abdominal wall, ensuring the re-approximation of the healthy tissue for defect closure.

The surgeon introduces a ruler to measure the lateral sidewall hernia space, determining it to be approximately seventeen by twenty centimeters. A macroporous polypropylene mesh is then cut to fit the measured space, ensuring proper orientation and size.
The surgeon performs a standard peritoneal flap closure without using trans-fascial fixation. The area where the sac was abandoned is also being closed.
